serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grilled beef chuck steak is approximately 150 g, representing one medium-sized steak portion as commonly served at a meal.

HPB values take precedence for all core macros.

No GI testing is required or available for pure protein-and-fat foods. glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 to satisfy template numeric requirement; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to convey the clinical interpretation.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~6.0 g/100 g) approaching AHA threshold; sodium is low at 51 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g, choose the leaner chuck cut with visible fat trimmed, and avoid adding salt, butter, or high-sodium sauces during preparation, as the saturated fat load at 6.0 g/100 g approaches the AHA 5 g/100 g threshold associated with arterial stiffness risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar; high protein (31.2 g/100 g) supports satiety without raising blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled beef chuck steak contains 0 g carbohydrates and 0 g sugar per 100 g and does not raise postprandial blood glucose; individuals with type 2 diabetes may include it in a balanced meal plan while monitoring total caloric and saturated fat intake, and should avoid sweet marinades or glazes that would add carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g (naturally occurring ruminant trans fat) elevate LDL-C risk; cholesterol 55 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit grilled beef chuck steak to occasional consumption (no more than 1–2 times per week), trim all visible fat before grilling, keep portions to 100 g or less, and pair with fibre-rich vegetables to mitigate the LDL-raising effect of the 6.0 g/100 g saturated fat and naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid in red meat amplifies RA inflammatory pathways; saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g exceeds 5 g threshold; naturally occurring trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit beef chuck steak due to its arachidonic acid content, elevated saturated fat (6.0 g/100 g), and naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), all of which amplify inflammatory pathways; replacing with fatty fish such as salmon or mackerel is recommended for anti-inflammatory protein intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled beef chuck steak is a cut of red meat taken from the shoulder (chuck) portion of beef cattle, typically bone-in, grilled over direct heat until cooked through with a slightly charred exterior. The chuck is a well-exercised muscle group, yielding a flavourful but moderately tough cut with visible intramuscular fat marbling and external fat attached. Grilling renders much of the surface fat and produces a caramelised, smoky crust via Maillard reaction. Per 100 g, it provides 260 kcal, 31.2 g protein, 15.1 g total fat (including approximately 6.0 g saturated fat and 0.2 g naturally occurring ruminant trans fat), 0 g carbohydrates, 51 mg sodium, 286 mg potassium, 6 mg calcium, and 2.98 mg iron. It is a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Clinically, the saturated fat content warrants moderation for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when consumed without sweet marinades. Commonly served as a main course in Western cuisine, it pairs well with non-starchy vegetables, salads, or grilled mushrooms. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database record F06030024 with confidence 0.95 and review status 'reviewed'. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for beef chuck (cooked), which is an approved secondary source. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for a grilled bone-in beef chuck cut.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as grilled beef chuck steak is a pure animal protein and fat product containing no carbohydrate-bearing ingredients. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a meat product with no plant-derived fibre. Glycemic index is set to numeric 0 to satisfy template numeric field requirement; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to convey the correct clinical interpretation that GI testing is not applicable for a food containing negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g). GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. Saturated fat value of 6.0 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13360 (beef chuck, arm pot roast, lean and fat, cooked) as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid breakdown; this is documented as an adapted match. Trans fat value of ~0.2 g/100 g represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid precursors) consistent with USDA data for cooked beef chuck; this is not industrially produced trans fat. Monounsaturated fat 6.4 g/100 g and polyunsaturated fat 0.6 g/100 g are adapted from USDA SR Legacy for the same cut. Fields set to -1 (data not available from approved sources for this specific preparation): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB grilled chuck steak preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo record; USDA SR Legacy values for the analogous cut exist but are not mapped here to avoid mixing source precision levels across all micronutrient fields. This food is classified as Western cuisine; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable and are set to null as this is not a Singaporean dish. The occasion tag 'breakfast' from the input has been noted but not carried forward as grilled chuck steak is not a conventional breakfast item in any primary cuisine context; meal_context reflects more accurate usage.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Grilled Beef Chuck Steak (F06030024) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The sole failure in iteration 2 was q4_glycemic_completeness requiring glycemic_index to be numeric; this has been resolved by setting glycemic_index to 0 (numeric sentinel) with gi_category 'not_applicable' to preserve correct clinical interpretation for a zero-carbohydrate food. Primary nutrient data sourced from HPB Foodo F06030024 (confidence 0.95, reviewed). Fatty acid breakdown adapted from USDA SR Legacy for beef chuck (cooked) as approved secondary source. Zero-carbohydrate macros justified as naturally zero. Three required health conditions present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are defensible and documented. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent.
